figures.caption: Table 7: π3 [Wang et al., 2025b] evaluation results. Top: camera pose estimation on Co3Dv2 [Reizenstein et al., 2021] and Re10K [Zhou et al., 2018] (AUC, higher is better). Bottom: point map reconstruction on DTU [Jensen et al., 2014] and ETH3D [Schops et al., 2017] (Acc and Comp lower is better; N.C. higher is better). Best results in bold.

Table 1: Camera pose estimation results for VGGT on Co3Dv2 [Reizenstein et al., 2021] and Re10K [Zhou et al., 2018]. AUC metrics measure the area under the cumulative error curve at different thresholds (higher is better). Best results in bold.
Table 3: Point map reconstruction for VGGT on DTU [Jensen et al., 2014]. Best results in bold.

Table 2: Point map reconstruction results for VGGT on 7-Scenes [Shotton et al., 2013] and ETH3D [Schops et al., 2017]. Acc measures accuracy (higher is better), Comp measures completeness (lower is better), and N.C. measures normal consistency (higher is better). Best results in bold.

Figure 3: Fisher information accurately predicts quantization sensitivity and reveals task-specific patterns. (a) Per-block and per-channel quantization loss for camera pose estimation. (b) Per-block and per-channel quantization loss for depth estimation. The markedly quantization loss profiles between tasks motivate task-aware quantization strategies. (c) Fisher-predicted loss versus empirically measured loss across transformer blocks under W4A4 quantization, showing strong correlation (r = 0.88).
Figure 7: Visual comparison results for π3 on 7-Scenes [Shotton et al., 2013] for Ground Truth, RTN W4A4, and FGQ W4A4.
Table 4: Depth estimation results on KITTI (monocular and video depth). Lower is better for AbsRel, SqRel, and RMSE; higher is better for δ < 1.25. Best results in bold.
Figure 5: Visual comparison results for VGGT on DTU [Jensen et al., 2014] for Ground Truth, QuantVGGT [Feng et al., 2025] W4A4, and FGQ W4A4.
